title: ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 Urine Analyzer - Strip Feed / Cassette Loading Error
description: Troubleshooting strip feed, cassette loading, strip detection, transport, or positioning errors before escalating the AUTION MAX AX-4030 for repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION MAX AX-4030 is not actively processing patient specimens before opening covers, checking the cassette area, or clearing a feed path. If testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated urine analyzer or follow the facility’s approved downtime procedure. Expected outcome: No patient result is delayed, lost, or reported from a device with an unresolved strip handling issue.

steps.title: Stop Additional Testing
steps.instructions: Ask staff not to load additional specimens or strips until the issue is understood. Confirm whether any patient sample was in process when the strip feed or cassette loading error occurred. Expected outcome: Questionable or incomplete results are identified and repeated according to laboratory policy.

steps.title: Verify the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves: Cassette not detected; Strip cassette difficult to insert; Strip feed error after cassette installation; Analyzer not pulling strips; Strip jam or misfeed; Repeated error with one cassette; Error after changing reagent strip lot; Error after cleaning or maintenance. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed as a strip feed or cassette loading issue, not a sample, printer, LIS, or QC issue.

steps.title: Check the Strip Cassette Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm the cassette is fully seated, installed in the correct orientation, and latched or closed as intended. Remove and reinstall the cassette using normal operator-accessible handling only. Expected outcome: If the cassette was misaligned or not fully seated, the analyzer recognizes it and the error clears. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Cassette for Damage or Obstruction
steps.instructions: Check the cassette for cracked plastic, warped areas, bent guides, stuck strips, debris, or signs of moisture exposure. Do not force the cassette into place if resistance is felt. Expected outcome: A damaged or obstructed cassette is identified as the cause and replaced. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Correct Reagent Strip Cassette and Lot
steps.instructions: Confirm laboratory staff are using the correct reagent strip cassette type for the AUTION MAX AX-4030. Check whether the issue began after a new cassette, new lot, or new shipment was opened. Expected outcome: Incorrect, expired, or suspect strips are removed from use before internal analyzer failure is suspected.

steps.title: Check Strip Storage and Handling Conditions
steps.instructions: Ask whether strips were exposed to humidity, heat, open storage, or improper handling. Moisture or warped strips can cause feeding, separation, or detection problems. Expected outcome: Environmental or handling-related strip issues are ruled out or corrected.

steps.title: Look for Loose Strips or Debris in the Loading Area
steps.instructions: With the analyzer safe and idle, inspect the accessible cassette loading area and strip path for loose strips, broken strip pieces, packaging debris, dust, or dried urine residue. Remove only visible, easily accessible debris. Do not disassemble internal mechanisms. Expected outcome: The feed path is clear of obvious obstruction. If debris removal resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Covers, Doors, and Interlocks
steps.instructions: Verify that all user-accessible covers, doors, and cassette access panels are fully closed. A partially closed cover may prevent cassette recognition or strip feeding. Expected outcome: The analyzer detects all covers properly and allows normal strip loading.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer Safely
steps.instructions: After confirming the analyzer is not processing specimens, power it down using the normal shutdown process if available. Wait briefly, then restart the analyzer and observe whether the initialization or cassette check completes normally. Expected outcome: A temporary detection or feed sequence fault clears. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Run an Operator-Level Initialization or Ready Check
steps.instructions: Allow the analyzer to complete its normal startup, initialization, or ready-state check. Watch for abnormal movement, repeated feed attempts, grinding, clicking, or immediate cassette-related errors. Expected outcome: The analyzer either returns to ready status or confirms the strip feed/cassette issue is still present.

steps.title: Test With a Known Good Cassette if Available
steps.instructions: If allowed by lab policy, have staff test with a different valid cassette or strip lot. Do not use patient testing to prove the repair. Use the facility’s approved QC or verification process. Expected outcome: If the second cassette works, the original cassette or strip lot is likely the issue. If the error remains, the analyzer likely requires service.

steps.title: Check for Recent Maintenance or Cleaning Activity
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after cleaning, cassette replacement, spill cleanup, relocation, or routine maintenance. Confirm all removed user-accessible parts were reinstalled correctly. Expected outcome: A reassembly or handling issue is corrected before escalation.

steps.title: Verify the Final Device Status
steps.instructions: If the analyzer reaches ready status and passes required lab checks, document the result and return it to service per facility policy. If the error repeats, do not keep cycling strips through the analyzer. Expected outcome: The device is either safely returned to service or removed for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 displayed a strip feed error after loading a reagent strip cassette.
CCR.Cause: Cassette area inspection found the strip cassette was not fully seated and the analyzer could not complete strip detection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the cassette, verified covers were closed, restarted the analyzer, and confirmed the unit returned to ready status with no repeat cassette loading error.

helpfulDetails: Exact strip feed or cassette loading message displayed
Whether the issue started after a new cassette or strip lot
Whether the cassette was reseated or replaced
Whether loose strips or debris were found
Any unusual clicking, grinding, or repeated feed attempts
Whether a power cycle cleared the issue
Whether QC or verification passed after troubleshooting
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 Urine Analyzer - Sample Aspiration / Pipetting Fault
description: Troubleshooting sample aspiration, pipetting, probe, tubing, cup position, or fluid handling faults before removing the analyzer from service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION MAX AX-4030 is not actively processing patient specimens before opening covers, checking probes, handling racks, or restarting the analyzer. If urine testing is needed urgently, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not interrupt active patient testing or risk questionable urine results.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Confirm Result Status
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether a patient specimen was being processed when the aspiration or pipetting fault occurred. Confirm whether the result was completed, aborted, flagged, or must be repeated according to lab policy. Expected outcome: No incomplete or unreliable urine result is released.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Review the displayed error or staff report. Confirm whether the issue involves: No sample aspiration; Insufficient sample volume; Probe not reaching the sample; Pipetting error during test sequence; Air bubbles in tubing; Repeated failure on one rack or cup type; Intermittent aspiration faults; Fault after reagent, waste, or maintenance activity. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed as a sample aspiration or pipetting issue, not a rack loading, barcode, LIS, strip, or QC problem.

steps.title: Check Sample Volume and Cup Position
steps.instructions: Verify with staff that the urine sample volume meets the analyzer’s required minimum for the cup or tube being used. Confirm the specimen cup or tube is seated correctly in the rack and is not tilted, overfilled, underfilled, or blocked by a cap. Expected outcome: The analyzer has a properly positioned sample that the probe can safely access. If correcting the sample volume or cup position resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the correction.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Probe Obstruction
steps.instructions: With the analyzer idle and safe to access, visually inspect the sample probe area for dried urine, crystals, debris, bent probe appearance, or physical obstruction. Do not force, bend, or manually realign the probe. Expected outcome: The probe path is clear and there is no obvious external blockage. If visible contamination is present, follow the facility-approved operator cleaning process or manufacturer-approved external cleaning procedure.

steps.title: Check for Clotted, Foamy, or Contaminated Samples
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the problem occurs with one specimen or multiple specimens. Urine samples with heavy sediment, foam, crystals, or debris may cause aspiration problems or volume detection issues. Expected outcome: A sample-quality issue is ruled out before assuming analyzer failure. If the issue follows one poor-quality specimen, have staff recollect, remix, or repeat per laboratory policy.

steps.title: Verify Rack and Cup Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct rack, tube, or cup type is being used for the AUTION MAX AX-4030 workflow. Check that adapters are seated correctly and that the sample container height allows proper probe access. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not failing because of an incorrect container or rack setup.

steps.title: Check for External Fluid Path Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect user-accessible sample tubing, reagent tubing, rinse lines, and nearby fittings for obvious kinks, loose connections, pinch points, leaks, or air gaps. Do not disconnect internal fluid lines unless permitted by site procedure and training. Expected outcome: No simple external tubing issue is preventing aspiration or pipetting. If a loose or kinked external line is corrected and the issue clears, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Confirm Wash, Rinse, and Waste Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify required wash or rinse fluids are present and correctly installed. Confirm waste containers are not full and waste tubing is not kinked or blocked. Aspiration systems may fault if rinse, wash, or waste handling cannot complete properly. Expected outcome: The analyzer has the fluid support needed to complete pipetting and probe cleaning.

steps.title: Check for Air Bubbles or Loss of Prime
steps.instructions: Look for visible air in accessible tubing or repeated aspiration errors after reagent replacement, fluid bottle replacement, cleaning, or extended idle time. Have qualified staff perform the analyzer’s approved prime, rinse, or fluid preparation routine if available through normal operation. Expected outcome: The fluid path is restored without internal disassembly. If priming clears the fault and test performance is verified, stop troubleshooting and document the action.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the fault continues after external checks, perform a controlled restart only when the analyzer is idle and no patient specimen is in process. Allow the system to complete startup checks before testing again. Expected outcome: Temporary control or sensor communication issues are cleared.

steps.title: Run an Approved Verification Check
steps.instructions: After correcting an external issue, have laboratory staff run QC, control material, or an approved verification process before returning the analyzer to patient testing. Expected outcome: The analyzer demonstrates acceptable aspiration and result performance before clinical use resumes.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 was generating sample aspiration faults during urine testing.
CCR.Cause: Found the sample rack loaded with underfilled urine cups, preventing reliable probe aspiration.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected sample volume and cup placement, had staff repeat QC verification, and returned the analyzer to service after successful testing.

helpfulDetails: Error message or fault code displayed
Whether the issue affected one sample or all samples
Cup, tube, rack, or adapter type verified
Tubing checked for kinks, leaks, or air bubbles
Waste bottle or drain condition checked
QC or verification result after troubleshooting

title: ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 Urine Analyzer - Waste Bottle or Drain Error
description: Troubleshooting waste bottle, drain tubing, liquid waste, overflow, leakage, or drain detection issues on the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION MAX AX-4030 is not actively processing patient samples before inspecting the waste bottle, tubing, rear connections, or drain path. If testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated urine analyzer or follow the approved downtime procedure. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not interrupt active testing or risk incomplete patient results.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Confirm Result Status
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether a patient specimen was in process when the waste or drain error occurred. Confirm whether the result completed, was aborted, or needs to be repeated according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: No questionable urine chemistry result is released.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Waste or Drain Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves: Waste bottle full; Drain bottle not detected; Drain error during measurement; Liquid not draining; Waste fluid backing up; Leak near the bottle or rear tubing; Error after emptying or replacing the bottle; Error after cleaning or maintenance. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a waste or drain problem, not a general fluidics, power, strip transport, or sample aspiration issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Waste Bottle Level
steps.instructions: Check whether the waste bottle contains liquid waste and is full or near full. The AX-4030 operating information identifies liquid waste in the drain bottle as something to check and discard when present. Empty the waste bottle only using the laboratory’s approved PPE and biohazard waste process. Expected outcome: If the waste bottle was full, emptying and reinstalling it should clear the error. If the issue clears, verify operation and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Waste Bottle Is Properly Seated
steps.instructions: Make sure the bottle is placed in its normal position and the cap, tubing, and sensor connection are not pulled loose, cross-threaded, pinched, or hanging under tension. Expected outcome: The analyzer recognizes the waste bottle and has a clear drain path.

steps.title: Check the Rear Drain Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the drain tubing connection at the rear of the analyzer. Reference documentation identifies a drain bottle tube connection at the rear panel drain joint. Confirm the tubing is connected securely and routed to the correct waste bottle. Expected outcome: A loose or disconnected drain line is corrected before further testing.

steps.title: Inspect Drain Tubing for Kinks or Blockage
steps.instructions: Follow the visible drain tubing from the analyzer to the waste bottle. Look for: Kinked tubing; Pinched tubing behind the analyzer; Tubing trapped under the bottle; Dried residue or crystallization; Liquid trapped in a low loop; Leaking or cracked tubing. Expected outcome: The drain path is open, supported, and free of obvious external restriction.

steps.title: Check for Leaks or Spills
steps.instructions: Inspect around the waste bottle, tubing, rear connections, and bench surface. If liquid is present, stop troubleshooting until the area is cleaned using the facility’s biohazard process. Expected outcome: No active leak remains, and the analyzer is safe to handle.

steps.title: Confirm the Waste Bottle Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: If the waste bottle uses a level or detection sensor connection, verify the cable is fully seated and not wet, damaged, stretched, or unplugged. Do not bypass sensors. Expected outcome: The analyzer can correctly detect waste bottle status.

steps.title: Check Washing Solution and Drain Relationship
steps.instructions: Confirm the washing solution bottle is not empty and that its tubing is not confused with the drain tubing. A waste or drain error may occur alongside fluid handling issues if the analyzer cannot properly wash, rinse, or drain during operation. Expected outcome: Supply and waste tubing are correctly routed and the analyzer has both fluid input and drain capacity.

steps.title: Look for Recent Maintenance or Bottle Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after: Emptying the waste bottle; Replacing tubing; Moving the analyzer; Cleaning the washing bath; Refilling washing solution; Performing maintenance; Repositioning the analyzer on the bench. Expected outcome: Any maintenance-related seating, routing, or reconnection issue is corrected.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the waste bottle has been emptied, tubing is secure, no leak is present, and no patient sample is in process, restart the analyzer according to normal facility practice. Expected outcome: The analyzer completes startup without the waste or drain error returning.

steps.title: Run an Approved Check or Non-Patient Verification
steps.instructions: After the external issue is corrected, have the lab perform the appropriate approved verification, check, or QC process before returning the analyzer to patient testing. Expected outcome: The analyzer drains normally and is acceptable for clinical use.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Patient Testing if the Error Returns
steps.instructions: If the waste or drain error returns after the bottle is emptied, tubing is checked, connections are secure, and no external leak or obstruction is found, stop troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The device is removed from service for further evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 displayed a waste bottle/drain error during urine analyzer operation.
CCR.Cause: Waste bottle was found full and the drain tubing was slightly kinked behind the analyzer, preventing proper drainage.
CCR.Resolution: Emptied the waste bottle using proper biohazard handling, corrected tubing routing, restarted the analyzer, and had the lab complete acceptable verification before returning the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Error occurred during startup, standby, or measurement

title: ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 Urine Analyzer - Calibration / QC / Abnormal Recovery Failure
description: Troubleshooting calibration failure, QC failure, abnormal chemistry recovery, or lot-related urine strip result concerns on the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION MAX AX-4030 is not being used to release patient results while calibration, QC, or recovery performance is questionable. If testing is needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: No patient results are reported from an analyzer with unresolved QC or calibration concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what specifically failed. Check whether the issue involves calibration failure, QC out of range, abnormal recovery, failed check strip, failed control level, lot setup issue, or unexpected chemistry pad results. Expected outcome: The problem is identified as calibration, QC, lot setup, reagent strip, control material, or analyzer performance related.

steps.title: Stop Patient Testing Until QC Is Acceptable
steps.instructions: Do not allow patient testing to continue if QC is outside the laboratory’s acceptable limits. Confirm whether any patient results were generated after the failed QC or calibration event. Expected outcome: Questionable results are reviewed by laboratory leadership according to facility policy.

steps.title: Review the Failure Pattern
steps.instructions: Determine whether the failure affects one QC level, multiple QC levels, one chemistry pad, all chemistry pads, one strip lot, or all testing. A single-level or single-pad failure may point toward control material, strip handling, or lot setup. Broad failure may suggest calibration, contamination, environmental, or analyzer-related issues. Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps avoid assuming an internal analyzer fault too early.

steps.title: Check Reagent Strip Lot and Expiration
steps.instructions: Verify the installed or selected strip lot matches the reagent strip container in use. Confirm the strips are not expired and were stored according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Incorrect lot selection, expired strips, or improperly stored strips are ruled out.

steps.title: Check QC Material Lot, Expiration, and Handling
steps.instructions: Confirm the QC material is within expiration, properly mixed if required, and stored according to the manufacturer and lab procedure. Ask whether the QC bottle was recently opened, replaced, or left uncapped. Expected outcome: Control degradation, contamination, or wrong QC material is ruled out.

steps.title: Verify Calibration or Check Material Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the calibration or check strip/material is correct for the AUTION MAX AX-4030 and is not expired, damaged, contaminated, or mismatched to the current setup. Expected outcome: The analyzer is being checked with the correct, valid material.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Loading and Transport Area
steps.instructions: Confirm strips are feeding, advancing, and positioning correctly. Look for misloaded strips, residue, dried urine, control material buildup, dust, or debris in the strip path and accessible tray areas. Expected outcome: Poor strip positioning or contamination is ruled out as a cause of abnormal recovery.

steps.title: Check Sample and QC Application Workflow
steps.instructions: Ask staff how QC was applied to the strip or introduced to the analyzer. Verify timing, sample volume, mixing, strip dipping, blotting, and loading are consistent with lab procedure. Expected outcome: Operator timing or application error is ruled out.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue began after a new strip lot, QC lot, calibration material, cleaning, maintenance, software setting change, LIS update, or analyzer relocation. Expected outcome: A recent workflow or material change is identified if present.

steps.title: Inspect Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the analyzer is on a stable, level surface away from excessive heat, direct sunlight, vibration, moisture, or chemical fumes. Abnormal environmental conditions can affect reagent strips, optics, and recovery performance. Expected outcome: The analyzer environment is appropriate for reliable testing.

steps.title: Check Basic Power Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is connected to a stable power source and that the power cord is fully seated. Avoid shared outlets with high-load devices if instability is suspected. Expected outcome: Power instability is ruled out as a contributor to intermittent failures.

steps.title: Review Analyzer Status and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Check the display for active warnings, maintenance prompts, calibration messages, strip feed errors, optics-related errors, or QC lockout conditions. Expected outcome: Any related analyzer condition is documented before further action.

steps.title: Perform Approved User-Level Cleaning Only
steps.instructions: If contamination is visible, have the analyzer cleaned only according to approved facility or manufacturer user-level procedures. Do not perform deep disassembly or internal optics cleaning unless authorized and trained. Expected outcome: External contamination is corrected without creating additional risk.

steps.title: Repeat QC After Corrective Action
steps.instructions: After correcting any obvious external issue, have laboratory staff repeat QC using valid strips and valid QC material. Expected outcome: QC passes and the analyzer may return to service only if laboratory policy allows.

steps.title: Compare With Another Validated Method if Needed
steps.instructions: If recovery remains questionable, ask the lab to compare results using another validated analyzer or approved backup process. Expected outcome: Patient testing continuity is maintained while the AX-4030 remains under evaluation.

steps.title: Remove From Service if QC or Calibration Still Fails
steps.instructions: If valid strips, valid QC, proper workflow, cleaning, environment, and setup have been confirmed but failures continue, remove the analyzer from service. Expected outcome: The device is not used clinically until repair, service evaluation, or further bench testing is completed.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported repeated QC failure and abnormal urine chemistry recovery on the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030.
CCR.Cause: Found expired QC material in use and confirmed the analyzer passed QC after valid control material was prepared and tested.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced QC material, verified strip lot setup, repeated QC successfully, and returned the AUTION MAX AX-4030 to service.

helpfulDetails: QC level or chemistry pad that failed
Whether one level or all levels failed
Whether one pad or multiple pads were affected
Recent reagent, QC, cleaning, or software changes

title: ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 Urine Analyzer - LIS / Host Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting AX-4030 result transmission, host interface, LIS, middleware, cable, or communication setup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION MAX AX-4030 is not actively processing patient specimens before restarting the analyzer, disconnecting cables, or changing communication settings. If testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the facility’s approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are not interrupted during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Communication Failure
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what is failing. Check whether the issue involves: Results not reaching the LIS; Results stuck on the analyzer; Manual result send failure; Host communication error; Worklist download failure; Intermittent transmission delay; LIS receiving incomplete or rejected results. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a host/LIS communication problem, not a barcode, sample ID, QC, printer, or analyzer testing issue.

steps.title: Verify Results Are Stored Locally
steps.instructions: Confirm that patient or QC results are still available on the analyzer before restarting or clearing anything. Do not delete, overwrite, or reset stored results unless the lab confirms they have been captured or documented according to policy. Expected outcome: No patient results are lost during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Status and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer screen for host, communication, interface, or transmission-related messages. Also check whether the analyzer is in a ready state or stopped due to another error, such as rack transport, strip handling, QC lockout, or reagent issue. Expected outcome: Communication troubleshooting does not continue if the analyzer is actually stopped for a separate operational fault.

steps.title: Confirm Network or Host Connection Type
steps.instructions: Identify how the AX-4030 is connected to the host system. Depending on site setup, this may involve a serial/RS-232 style interface, middleware connection, data manager, or networked interface device. Expected outcome: The correct communication path is identified before cables or settings are changed.

steps.title: Inspect External Communication Cables
steps.instructions: Check the host communication cable for loose connectors, bent pins, damaged strain relief, disconnected adapters, or labeling issues. Reseat the cable at the analyzer and at the host, converter, wall jack, terminal server, or middleware connection point if accessible. Expected outcome: A loose or damaged external connection is corrected. If communication resumes, verify result transmission and stop.

steps.title: Check Power to External Interface Hardware
steps.instructions: If the analyzer uses an external serial converter, terminal server, network adapter, or middleware interface box, confirm it has power and normal indicator lights. Look for failed power supplies, unplugged adapters, no link light, unusual heat, or error indicators. Expected outcome: External interface hardware is powered and appears functional.

steps.title: Verify Network Jack or Host Port Status
steps.instructions: If a network connection is used, check for link lights at the wall jack, switch port, converter, or interface device. If allowed by facility policy, test with a known-good cable or known-working network jack assigned for that device type. Expected outcome: A bad patch cable, inactive jack, or disconnected network path is ruled out.

steps.title: Confirm No Recent Changes Occurred
steps.instructions: Ask staff or IT whether anything recently changed, including: LIS downtime; Middleware update; Interface engine change; IP address change; Port change; Serial-to-network converter replacement; Network switch work; Analyzer relocation; Barcode or sample ID workflow change. Expected outcome: Recent environmental or system changes are identified before suspecting analyzer failure.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Host Settings Against Site Records
steps.instructions: Review the AX-4030 host communication settings only if Clinical Engineering is authorized to do so. Compare visible settings to site documentation, such as host enabled status, communication mode, baud rate, parity, stop bits, device ID, IP address, port, or destination host. Expected outcome: Settings match the approved site configuration. If a mismatch is found and corrected per local process, send a test result and stop if successful.

steps.title: Verify LIS or Middleware Availability
steps.instructions: Contact the LIS, middleware, or IT interface team to confirm the receiving system is online and accepting results. Ask whether the analyzer is connecting, whether messages are arriving, and whether results are being rejected due to patient ID, accession number, operator ID, QC status, or formatting. Expected outcome: The failure is separated into analyzer communication failure, middleware downtime, LIS rejection, or workflow/data issue.

steps.title: Send a Controlled Test Transmission
steps.instructions: Using a non-patient test result, QC result, or approved test record, attempt a manual resend if available and permitted. Have the lab or LIS team confirm whether the message is received. Expected outcome: Transmission success or failure is verified without risking patient result integrity.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If external checks are complete and results are protected, restart the analyzer and any external interface device according to local downtime procedures. Do not power-cycle during active testing or while unsent patient results are at risk. Expected outcome: Temporary communication lockup clears. If transmission resumes, verify with the LIS team and return the device to service.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Intermittent Failures
steps.instructions: If communication restores but drops again, document timing, frequency, error messages, link light behavior, and whether failure occurs after specific workflows. Expected outcome: Intermittent failures are documented clearly for IT, LIS, vendor support, or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Do Not Perform Internal Communication Board Repair in the Field
steps.instructions: If cables, power, external interface hardware, network path, settings, and LIS availability are all verified, avoid internal disassembly or board-level troubleshooting during clinical support. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting stops at the appropriate Clinical Engineering boundary.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that the ARKRAY AUTION MAX AX-4030 was completing urine tests, but results were not transmitting to the LIS.
CCR.Cause: Communication cable and analyzer status were checked; external interface device had no link indication at the network connection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the network patch cable at the interface device, restored link indication, confirmed successful test result transmission with the LIS team, and returned analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact host or communication message displayed
Wall jack or network link light status
External interface box power and indicator lights
Any recent network, middleware, or analyzer relocation changes

title: ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 Urine Analyzer - Strip Placement / Timing Error
description: Troubleshooting strip placement, timing, alignment, detection, and read-start errors on the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is not actively processing a patient urine strip before troubleshooting. If testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime procedure. Expected outcome: No patient result is delayed, lost, or reported from a questionable strip run.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether a patient strip was already dipped or placed when the error occurred. Confirm whether the test was completed, rejected, or needs to be repeated according to lab policy. Expected outcome: Incomplete or improperly timed urine chemistry results are not released.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves: Strip placed too early; Strip placed too late; Analyzer not detecting the strip; Strip not aligned correctly; Repeated timing prompts; Read cycle starting but failing; Error only with certain operators; Error only with certain strip bottles. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a strip placement or timing problem, not a QC, printer, LIS, or power issue.

steps.title: Verify Correct Test Strip Type
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using ARKRAY AUTION Sticks intended for the AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022. The AE-4022 is designed around AUTION strip technology and automatic strip reading, so incorrect or incompatible strips can cause placement, alignment, or reading problems. Expected outcome: The correct strips are being used before analyzer failure is suspected.

steps.title: Check Strip Condition and Handling
steps.instructions: Inspect the strip bottle and several unused strips. Look for: Expired strips; Moisture exposure; Warped strips; Bent reagent pads; Discolored pads; Bottle left open; Strips stored outside required conditions. Expected outcome: Damaged or improperly stored strips are ruled out.

steps.title: Review Operator Timing Workflow
steps.instructions: Observe the operator’s normal process if possible. Confirm the strip is dipped, blotted, and placed according to the facility’s approved procedure. The AE-4022 supports continuous strip placement, and ARKRAY describes auto start mode with strips placed every 7 seconds, so incorrect pacing can contribute to timing complaints. Expected outcome: The operator is not placing strips too slowly, too quickly, or outside the expected analyzer workflow.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Placement Area
steps.instructions: With the analyzer idle, inspect the strip placement and transport area for obvious external issues. Look for: Dried urine residue; Control material buildup; Dust or debris; Stray strip fragments; Crystallized sample; Anything blocking strip seating or movement. Expected outcome: The strip can sit flat and enter the measurement path without obstruction.

steps.title: Clean User-Accessible Surfaces
steps.instructions: Clean only the user-accessible strip placement area using the facility-approved method and manufacturer-approved cleaning guidance. Do not flood the analyzer or use aggressive cleaning around sensors or openings. Expected outcome: Residue that could interfere with strip detection or alignment is removed.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Is Level and Stable
steps.instructions: Check that the AE-4022 is sitting on a stable, level bench with adequate clearance. A tilted or unstable surface can affect strip placement and movement on small benchtop analyzers. Expected outcome: The analyzer is physically positioned for reliable strip handling.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer if Safe
steps.instructions: If no specimens are in process, power the analyzer down and restart it. Allow it to complete startup checks before placing another strip. Expected outcome: Temporary software or detection state issues are cleared.

steps.title: Run a Controlled Test With a Fresh Strip
steps.instructions: Have lab staff test using a fresh, properly stored strip and approved control material if appropriate. Observe whether the strip is accepted, aligned, and read normally. The AE-4022 is designed to automatically align placed strips, so repeated failure after correct placement may indicate a detection, transport, or alignment problem. Expected outcome: If the strip is accepted and read correctly, return the analyzer to service after lab verification.

steps.title: Check for Pattern by Operator, Strip Lot, or Location
steps.instructions: If the issue is intermittent, compare failures by: Operator; Strip lot; Strip bottle; Time of day; Analyzer location; Cleaning history; Recent reagent change; Recent software or LIS workflow change. Expected outcome: A workflow, reagent, or environmental pattern is identified before repair escalation.

steps.title: Verify QC Status Before Patient Testing Resumes
steps.instructions: Confirm QC is acceptable before returning the analyzer to patient use. Do not return the analyzer to routine testing if QC is locked out, failed, expired, or incomplete. Expected outcome: Patient testing resumes only after the analyzer and testing process are verified.

steps.title: Stop if the Error Repeats Under Correct Conditions
steps.instructions: If the same strip placement or timing error occurs with correct strips, proper workflow, clean placement area, and acceptable setup, remove the analyzer from use. Expected outcome: The device is not used when a possible internal sensor, transport, or alignment issue remains unresolved.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported repeated strip placement/timing errors on the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 during routine urine testing.
CCR.Cause: Found dried residue in the strip placement area and confirmed one partially warped strip bottle was being used.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned user-accessible strip placement area, removed questionable strips from use, verified proper strip placement workflow, and analyzer passed control testing before return to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue occurred before or after strip placement
Strip lot and expiration date checked
QC status before and after troubleshooting
Whether fresh strips resolved the issue
Whether analyzer was returned to service or removed for repair

title: ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 Urine Analyzer - Optics / Reflectance Reading Fault
description: Troubleshooting reflectance read errors, abnormal strip readings, optical faults, contamination, strip placement issues, or external conditions affecting AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 results.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 is not actively processing a patient urine strip before troubleshooting. If patient testing is needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: No patient result is reported from an analyzer with a possible optical or reflectance reading issue.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether a patient strip was being read when the fault occurred. Confirm whether the result completed, was flagged, rejected, or needs to be repeated according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Questionable urine chemistry results are not released.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves: Reflectance or optical read error; Abnormal or inconsistent chemistry results; Failed strip reading; Error during startup or self-check; Failure only with one strip lot; Failure after cleaning, maintenance, or reagent change. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a reading or optical problem, not a printer, LIS, strip feed, or operator workflow issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Area for Contamination
steps.instructions: Check the strip tray, strip guide, and reading area for urine residue, dried control material, dust, paper fibers, or reagent buildup. Follow the facility-approved cleaning process for the accessible strip path and read area. Do not use excessive liquid or harsh cleaners. Expected outcome: Any visible contamination is removed, and the optical path is not being affected by residue.

steps.title: Check Strip Placement and Orientation
steps.instructions: Confirm the reagent strip is placed correctly on the strip tray and is not tilted, curled, reversed, or misaligned. Verify staff are using the correct strip type approved for the AE-4022. Expected outcome: The analyzer can read the strip pads consistently without position-related errors.

steps.title: Check Strip Condition and Storage
steps.instructions: Inspect the reagent strip bottle for expiration date, open-date requirements, moisture exposure, discoloration, or damaged pads. Confirm the cap is being closed promptly and strips are stored according to lab policy. Expected outcome: Strip degradation or improper storage is ruled out before suspecting the analyzer.

steps.title: Run a Known Good Strip or Control Material
steps.instructions: Have lab staff run QC or a known acceptable test strip according to the laboratory’s approved procedure. Compare whether the fault occurs again with properly handled materials. Expected outcome: If the analyzer reads normally, the issue may have been related to strip handling, sample condition, or operator technique.

steps.title: Check for Ambient Light or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is not placed in direct sunlight, under intense task lighting, near strong glare, or in an unstable environment. Also check for vibration, condensation, unusual heat, or recent relocation. Expected outcome: External environmental conditions are not interfering with reflectance measurement.

steps.title: Verify the Strip Tray Moves Smoothly
steps.instructions: Observe the strip tray during a test or allowed check cycle. Look for hesitation, binding, incomplete travel, unusual noise, or strip movement during the reading process. Expected outcome: The strip is being positioned correctly under the read area.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer When Safe
steps.instructions: Once all specimens are cleared and staff confirm testing can pause, power the analyzer off, wait briefly, and power it back on. Allow the analyzer to complete startup checks. Expected outcome: Temporary logic or sensor errors clear without returning.

steps.title: Repeat QC After Cleaning and Restart
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff repeat QC or the facility-approved performance check. Do not return the analyzer to patient use unless QC passes and the analyzer reads consistently. Expected outcome: Analyzer performance is verified before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Patterned Failure
steps.instructions: If the same optical or reflectance fault returns after cleaning, proper strips, correct placement, QC, and restart, document the pattern. Note whether the error occurs at startup, during every strip read, intermittently, or only on specific pads. Expected outcome: Common external causes have been ruled out and the failure pattern is ready for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 was producing a reflectance reading fault during urine strip testing.
CCR.Cause: Strip tray and read area were inspected; dried residue was found near the accessible strip path, and QC failed prior to cleaning.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned accessible strip path per approved process, verified proper strip placement, restarted analyzer, and had lab repeat QC; analyzer passed QC and was returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact optical or reflectance error displayed
Whether issue occurred during startup, QC, or patient testing
QC results before and after troubleshooting
Any unusual noise during tray movement
Any unusual heat, smell, glare, or environmental concern

title: ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 Urine Analyzer - Printer / Paper Feed Issue
description: Troubleshooting printer, paper feed, blank printout, jam, or report output problems on the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 is not actively processing a patient urine strip before opening the printer area, replacing paper, or restarting the analyzer. If patient testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the facility’s approved downtime documentation process. Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are not compromised during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Printer Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Confirm whether the issue is: No printout after testing; Paper not feeding; Paper jam; Blank printout; Faded or incomplete report; Printer noise with no paper movement; Results visible on screen but not printed; Printing failure after paper replacement. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a printer or paper feed problem, not a testing, LIS, operator workflow, or result storage issue.

steps.title: Confirm Results Are Still Available
steps.instructions: Check whether the urine result is still visible on the analyzer display or stored in memory. If the result is needed for patient care, have laboratory staff document or transmit it according to lab policy before further troubleshooting. Expected outcome: No completed patient result is lost due to printer troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Printer Paper Supply
steps.instructions: Open the printer cover and verify that paper is installed. Confirm the roll is not empty, crushed, wet, contaminated, or installed loosely. Replace the paper roll if it is low, damaged, or questionable. Expected outcome: The printer has usable paper available for feeding and printing.

steps.title: Verify Paper Orientation
steps.instructions: Confirm the thermal paper is loaded in the correct direction. Thermal printers only print on the coated side of the paper. If the paper is reversed, the analyzer may feed paper but produce a blank report. Expected outcome: Paper feeds with the printable thermal side correctly positioned.

steps.title: Check for Paper Jam or Torn Paper
steps.instructions: Inspect the paper path for torn paper, small scraps, folded edges, or debris. Remove only accessible paper fragments without forcing the printer mechanism. Do not use sharp tools that could damage the print head, roller, or paper sensor area. Expected outcome: The paper path is clear and the paper can advance normally.

steps.title: Reload the Paper Roll Cleanly
steps.instructions: Remove the paper roll and reload it neatly. Make sure the paper edge is straight and not curled, folded, or jammed under the cover. Close the printer cover fully. Expected outcome: The printer cover seats properly and the paper is positioned for normal feeding.

steps.title: Check for Cover or Door Not Fully Closed
steps.instructions: Confirm the printer cover is latched correctly. A loose or partially closed cover can prevent proper paper pressure against the print mechanism. Expected outcome: The printer cover is secure and paper feed pressure is restored.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Print Test
steps.instructions: Use an available analyzer print function, stored result print, or approved test report workflow if accessible. Do not use active patient testing just to test the printer. Expected outcome: The analyzer prints a readable report without paper slipping, jamming, or feeding incorrectly.

steps.title: Check Print Quality
steps.instructions: If the report prints but is faint, incomplete, or uneven, replace the paper roll with a known good thermal roll approved for the analyzer. Avoid using old, heat-exposed, damp, or non-thermal paper. Expected outcome: Print quality improves with known good paper.

steps.title: Check for External Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect around the printer opening for urine residue, control material, dust, or paper debris. Clean only external and user-accessible surfaces according to facility-approved cleaning practices. Do not spray cleaner directly into the printer. Expected outcome: External contamination is not interfering with paper movement or report readability.

steps.title: Verify Printer Settings or Output Selection
steps.instructions: Check whether printing has been disabled, set to manual print, or redirected through another result reporting workflow. Confirm with lab staff whether the analyzer is expected to print automatically after each test. Expected outcome: The printer behavior matches the laboratory’s intended workflow.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer Safely
steps.instructions: If the paper path is clear and paper is correctly loaded, restart the analyzer when it is safe to do so. Allow the analyzer to complete startup before attempting another print test. Expected outcome: Temporary printer or software lockups clear after restart.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Jam or Feed Failure
steps.instructions: If the same jam, slipping, or no-feed condition returns with known good paper and correct loading, suspect a printer roller, paper sensor, cover latch, motor, or internal printer assembly issue. Do not disassemble the printer mechanism beyond normal paper access. Expected outcome: Internal printer failure is suspected only after external causes are ruled out.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Successful Verification
steps.instructions: If the printer feeds correctly and produces a readable report, document the action and return the analyzer to service. If printing is required for the lab’s workflow, do not return the analyzer to normal use until report output is verified. Expected outcome: The AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 is returned to service only when result documentation is reliable.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 was not printing urine analyzer reports after testing.
CCR.Cause: Printer paper was installed backward, causing the analyzer to feed paper but produce blank printouts.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded thermal paper in the correct orientation, verified readable test print, confirmed stored result printout, and returned analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether results were visible on the analyzer display
Whether results were stored, printed, or transmitted
Any jammed or torn paper removed
Whether known good thermal paper was tested
Any printer noise during feed attempt
Whether the issue returned after restart
Environmental factors around the printer area such as moisture, dust, urine residue, control material, or paper debris
Indicator lights, screen messages, or printer prompts observed

title: ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 Urine Analyzer - QC Failure / Strip Lot Mismatch
description: Troubleshooting QC failure, QC lockout, strip lot mismatch, control recovery issues, or reagent strip setup problems on the AE-4022.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 is not being used to report active patient results while QC is failing or strip lot setup is questionable. If testing is needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: No patient results are released from an analyzer with unresolved QC or reagent strip concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported QC or Strip Lot Issue
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff what specifically occurred. Check whether the issue involves: QC failure; QC lockout; New strip lot not accepted; Control results outside range; Wrong strip type selected; Expired strip lot; Mismatch between bottle, analyzer setup, and QC material; Failure after opening a new bottle of strips; Repeated failure on one chemistry pad or multiple pads. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as a QC recovery, strip lot, setup, or workflow problem before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Strip Type Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using ARKRAY AUTION Sticks 10EA intended for the AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022. ARKRAY lists AUTION Sticks 10EA as the test strips for the AE-4022, and the analyzer uses reflectance photometry to read urine chemistry strips. Expected outcome: Incorrect, unsupported, or mixed strip types are ruled out. If the wrong strip type was loaded or selected, stop testing, correct the setup per lab procedure, repeat QC, and do not release results from the incorrect setup.

steps.title: Check Strip Bottle and Lot Information
steps.instructions: Compare the physical strip bottle label to the lot information entered or selected on the analyzer. Check: Lot number; Expiration date; Strip type; Open bottle date, if used by facility policy; Storage requirements; Whether strips from different bottles were mixed. Expected outcome: The analyzer setup matches the actual reagent strip bottle in use. If a lot mismatch is found, correct the lot setup according to facility procedure, run required QC, and stop if QC passes.

steps.title: Check QC Material Information
steps.instructions: Confirm the control material is correct for the AE-4022 urine strip method. Verify: Control lot number; Expiration date; Open vial date; Storage condition; Expected ranges; Control level being tested; Whether the correct QC level was selected on the analyzer. Expected outcome: QC material problems are ruled out before suspecting analyzer performance.

steps.title: Review QC Lockout or Operator Lockout Status
steps.instructions: Check whether the analyzer is preventing testing due to QC requirements, expired QC, failed QC, or operator ID restrictions. The AE-4022 supports QC lockout and operator ID lockout features, so a lockout may be a compliance setting rather than a hardware fault. Expected outcome: The lockout reason is understood and not mistaken for analyzer failure. If the analyzer is correctly locked out, have authorized laboratory personnel complete required QC or operator setup.

steps.title: Repeat QC With Fresh Materials
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff repeat QC using: Fresh control material; A properly stored strip bottle; Correct strip lot selection; Correct control level selection; Clean sample application technique. Expected outcome: QC passes when fresh, correctly matched materials are used. If QC passes, document the issue as reagent/control/setup related and return the analyzer to service per lab policy.

steps.title: Check Strip Handling and Timing
steps.instructions: Confirm strips are not being exposed to room air for extended periods before use. Verify staff are applying control material evenly and placing the strip correctly. The AE-4022 allows continuous strip placement and automatically aligns strips, but poor strip handling can still affect QC recovery. Expected outcome: Strip application, timing, and placement issues are ruled out.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Feed and Reading Area
steps.instructions: With testing stopped, inspect the user-accessible strip path for: Dried control material; Urine residue; Dust or debris; Misplaced strip; Sticky strip movement; Obstruction near the read area. Clean only according to approved operator-level cleaning procedures. Expected outcome: Contamination or obstruction is not affecting strip positioning or optical reading.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer, strips, and controls are being used in an appropriate lab environment. Look for: Excessive heat or cold; Direct sunlight; High humidity; Storage near sinks or vents; Controls or strips left uncapped; Recent relocation of the analyzer. Expected outcome: Environmental causes of abnormal QC recovery are ruled out.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the QC setup appears correct and the analyzer is idle, restart the analyzer. Allow it to complete startup checks before repeating QC. Expected outcome: Temporary software or workflow state issues clear without interrupting patient testing.

steps.title: Verify Final Status
steps.instructions: After corrective action, confirm: QC passes; Correct strip lot is active; QC lockout is cleared; No patient results were released during failed QC; Analyzer status is acceptable for use. Expected outcome: The analyzer is safe to return to service.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 failed QC after a new AUTION Sticks 10EA strip bottle was placed into use.
CCR.Cause: Strip lot entered on the analyzer did not match the lot number on the active reagent strip bottle.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected strip lot setup with laboratory staff, repeated QC successfully, verified QC lockout cleared, and returned analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds, heat, smell, or visible contamination

title: ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 Urine Analyzer - Operator ID / Barcode / Data Entry Issue
description: Troubleshooting operator ID, barcode scanning, specimen ID entry, and data entry issues before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 is not actively processing a patient urine strip before troubleshooting ID entry, scanner accessories, or workflow settings. If patient testing must continue, have staff use another validated analyzer or the facility’s approved downtime identification process. Expected outcome: No urine result is released under the wrong patient, specimen, or operator ID.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Entry Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what specifically is failing. Check whether the issue involves: Operator ID not accepted; Barcode scanner not reading; Specimen ID not scanning; Manual entry not working; Wrong characters appearing; Required ID field preventing testing; Intermittent scanner function; ID accepted on some samples but not others. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to operator ID, specimen ID, barcode scanner, keypad/data entry, or setup workflow.

steps.title: Stop and Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Verify whether any patient tests were run with missing, incorrect, or manually entered IDs. Ask laboratory staff to confirm whether affected results need correction, repeat testing, or LIS review according to policy. Expected outcome: Patient identification risk is addressed before further troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Barcode Label Quality
steps.instructions: Check several barcode labels that failed to scan. Look for: Wrinkled labels; Faded printing; Smudged ink; Torn barcodes; Curved labels on small tubes; Incorrect label placement; Low contrast printing; Barcode partly covered by tape or liquid. Poor label quality is a common cause of scanner complaints. Expected outcome: A clean, properly printed barcode should scan more reliably. If label quality was the issue, correct the labeling process and stop.

steps.title: Compare a Known-Good Barcode
steps.instructions: Test the scanner using a known-good barcode that is used successfully elsewhere in the lab. Use a non-patient test label or approved sample label per facility policy. Expected outcome: If the known-good barcode scans correctly, the analyzer and scanner are likely functioning, and the issue is with the failed labels or barcode format.

steps.title: Check Barcode Format Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the failed barcode type is supported by the scanner/analyzer setup. Ask whether there were recent changes to: LIS label format; Accession number length; Barcode symbology; Label printer; Middleware rules; Operator ID badge format; Leading or trailing characters. Expected outcome: If the problem began after a label or LIS change, escalate to lab IT, middleware, or LIS support before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Barcode Scanner Connection
steps.instructions: If an external barcode scanner is used, check that it is fully connected to the correct port. Look for loose cables, damaged connectors, bent pins, broken strain relief, or evidence that the scanner was dropped. Reseat the scanner connection if safe and appropriate. Expected outcome: A loose or partially connected scanner should resume normal function after reseating. If resolved, document and stop.

steps.title: Check Scanner Power or Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe the scanner during use. Confirm whether it: Illuminates or emits a scan beam; Beeps when scanning; Shows any indicator light; Sends characters to the analyzer; Fails completely with no response. Expected outcome: A scanner with no light, no beep, and no data output may have a cable, port, or scanner failure.

steps.title: Clean the Scanner Window
steps.instructions: Inspect the scanner lens or window for dust, fingerprints, urine splatter, residue, tape adhesive, or scratches. Clean only with approved methods and materials. Expected outcome: A scanner blocked by residue may read normally after cleaning. If resolved, return the device to service after verification.

steps.title: Check Manual Data Entry
steps.instructions: Attempt approved manual entry of an operator ID or specimen ID. Confirm that the keypad, touchscreen, or input buttons respond correctly. Expected outcome: If manual entry works but barcode scanning does not, focus on scanner, label quality, barcode format, or scanner connection.

steps.title: Verify Operator ID Is Valid
steps.instructions: Confirm with laboratory staff that the operator ID being entered is currently active and approved for analyzer use. Check whether the issue affects one user or all users. Expected outcome: If only one operator ID fails, the issue may be user setup, expired credentials, access rules, or LIS/operator management rather than analyzer hardware.

steps.title: Check for Required Field or Lockout Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the analyzer is preventing testing because a required operator ID, specimen ID, QC status, or sample field is missing. Ask whether the workflow was recently changed to require additional fields. Expected outcome: The analyzer may be operating correctly if it is enforcing required identification or QC rules.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer Safely
steps.instructions: If no specimen is processing, power cycle the analyzer using the approved shutdown and startup process. Do not restart during active testing or while results are being transmitted. Expected outcome: Temporary input or communication glitches may clear after restart. If resolved, verify scanner and manual entry before returning to service.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the problem started after: New barcode scanner installation; Scanner replacement; Label printer replacement; LIS or middleware update; Operator ID policy change; New accession label format; Analyzer relocation; Cleaning or spill event. Expected outcome: Recent changes help identify whether the cause is external workflow, accessory, configuration, or hardware related.

steps.title: Test With Another Compatible Scanner if Available
steps.instructions: If the facility has an approved compatible scanner, swap it temporarily for testing. Do not substitute an unapproved scanner for patient testing. Expected outcome: If the replacement scanner works, the original scanner or cable is likely faulty. Remove the failed scanner from service and replace or repair it.

steps.title: Verify Data Appears Correctly Before Use
steps.instructions: After any correction, scan or enter a test ID and confirm the displayed data is complete and accurate. Check for missing digits, extra characters, wrong prefix, wrong suffix, or field truncation. Expected outcome: The analyzer captures the correct ID before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Functional Verification
steps.instructions: Confirm successful operator ID entry, specimen ID entry, barcode scan behavior, and normal analyzer readiness. Document the final status in the work order. Expected outcome: The AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 is safe to return to service only after identification entry is reliable.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that the ARKRAY AUTION ELEVEN AE-4022 would not accept scanned operator IDs during urine testing.
CCR.Cause: Found barcode scanner window contaminated and several operator badge barcodes scanning intermittently.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned scanner window, verified scanner connection, confirmed successful scans with known-good operator and specimen barcodes, and returned analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether operator ID, specimen ID, or both failed
Barcode scanner light or beep behavior
Whether one user or all users were affected
Whether one barcode label type or all labels failed
Any extra, missing, or incorrect characters
